title: Regular and chaotic dynamics of nonlinear optomechanical systems controlled by modulated light
authors: A. P. Saiko, G. A. Rusetsky, S. A. Markevich, R. Fedaruk
abstract: The nonlinear dynamics of a mechanical resonator in an optomechanical system with linear, quadratic and cubic photon-vibration interactions (with respect to mechanical displacements) in a modulated driving field under conditions of adiabatic elimination of the optical field is studied. Based on the constructed bifurcation diagrams of the mechanical coordinate and the largest Lyapunov exponent as a function of the modulation amplitude, as well as power spectra, phase portraits and Poincare sections, regions of regular and chaotic dynamics of the optomechanical system are identified. It is also shown that for a certain modulation amplitude in the presence of all three types of interactions, chaotic dynamics of the mechanical resonator (oscillator) is realized, which is replaced by quasi-periodic oscillations in the absence of cubic interaction, and the system returns to chaotic behavior if only linear interaction remains. This non-monotonic dependence of chaotic dynamics on the order of nonlinearity originates from the interplay between parametric driving and effective potential reshaping and manifests that nonlinearity does not always enhance chaos. For an optomechanical system in a membrane-in-the-middle configuration, where only quadratic photon-vibration interaction is present, it is demonstrated that at small modulation amplitudes the mechanical oscillator exhibits quasi-periodic motion in each of the wells of a symmetric two-minimum potential, whereas large modulation amplitudes lead to chaotic motion, involving interwell transitions.
summary: This paper theoretically analyzes how nonlinearity influences the transition between regular and chaotic motion in optomechanical oscillators driven by modulated light. By mapping out phase space using Lyapunov exponents and Poincaré sections, the authors demonstrate that nonlinearity does not monotonically enhance chaos. The findings suggest controlling these systems near the edge of chaos for applications like sensing or random number generation.
main_problem: The study investigates the transition between regular and chaotic dynamics in nonlinear optomechanical systems subjected to modulated driving fields.
main_result: The system exhibits a non-monotonic dependence of chaos on nonlinearity order, and specific configurations (like membranes) show clear transitions from quasi-periodic motion to chaos, suggesting potential applications for sensing or random number generation.
method: The analysis employs advanced tools from dynamical systems theory, including constructing bifurcation diagrams, calculating the largest Lyapunov exponent, and analyzing Poincaré sections.
model_or_system: The model is an optomechanical system involving a mechanical resonator coupled to an optical field. The dynamics are governed by nonlinear interactions (linear, quadratic, cubic) between photons and mechanical displacements.
key_observables: Bifurcation diagrams ($\epsilon$ vs. dynamic regime), Largest Lyapunov exponent ($\lambda_{max}$), Power spectra, Phase portraits, and Poincaré sections.
important_parameters: Modulation amplitude/parameter ($\epsilon$), modulation frequency ($\Omega$), and the coupling strengths (linear $g$, quadratic $g'$, cubic $g''$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on the adiabatic elimination of the optical field variables, assuming the cavity decay rate is much larger than the mechanical resonance frequency.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate transitions between regimes: quasi-periodic motion (structured Poincaré points/discrete spectrum) vs. chaotic motion (randomly distributed points/continuous spectrum), particularly in membrane systems showing interwell chaos.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the dynamics by varying nonlinearity orders and control parameters ($\epsilon$), comparing results from different coupling terms, and detailing specific physical realizations like the membrane-in-the-middle configuration.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it applies nonlinear dynamics tools to a quantum optics platform (optomechanics), providing insights into chaos control and the engineering of non-classical states or noise sources using engineered potentials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
